glossary.definition: a field that deals with developing hardware and software solutions endowed with human-similar intellectual processes, such as the ability to reason, discover meaning, generalise, or learn from experience. It is a part of computer science.
The ability that is possessed by humans, animals, and even plants. It encompasses various manifestations such as learning, memory, perception, decision-making, and adaptation to the environment.
glossary.sentence: Nowadays, it is believed that humans, animals and even plants possess intelligence.

glossary.definition: Studied in-depth in many fields of science such as philosophy, linguistics, neuroscience, etc. Psychology has offered theories about its nature and characteristics, as well as tests for assessing intellectual development. However, there is no unified viewpoint on whether it is a single ability or a set of abilities, and measuring it is problematic due to diversity (linguistic, cultural, physical condition).
glossary.sentence: Human intelligence is studied in-depth in many fields of science, such as philosophy, linguistics, neuroscience, etc. However, it is psychology that has offered a set of theories about the nature of human intelligence and has defined many characteristics of human intelligence.

glossary.definition: Related to learning ability in animals such as monkeys, dolphins, elephants, rats, or crows. It is more challenging to measure than human intelligence due to greater diversity in cognitive and perceptual capacities, sensory organs, and methods of exploring the environment.
glossary.sentence: Much less we know about animal intelligence . It is mostly related to learning ability of such animals as monkeys, dolphins, elephant, rat, or crows. However it is even more challenging to measure animal intelligence than human intelligence, because animal diversity is even greater taking into account their cognitive and perceptual capacities, available sensory organs and the ways how they explore the environment in which they live.

glossary.definition: Not based on a brain and nervous system but possesses forms of intellectual performance such as learning from repeated practice, memory, simultaneous perception (shape, colour, smell, sound), and decision-making that reconciles well-being with the environment.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge about plant intelligence is even more limited. It is currently known that plant intelligence is not based on a brain and nervous system (Parise, Gagliano and Souza, 2020) . At the same time, plants possess many forms of intellectual performance, such as (Gagliano, 2015) : they learn based on repeated practice; they have memory; they are able to percept a number of aspects (shape, colour, smell and sound) at the same time; they have a “sense of place” and an awareness of the neighbourhood.

glossary.definition: Should encompass the essence of different types of intelligence, not be limited to specific senses, environments, goals, or implementation structures, be based on fundamental principles, and ideally be formally expressed, objective, and practically realisable as an effective test. However, it does not exist because human knowledge is still limited.
glossary.sentence: Thus, in order to cover the diversity of the existent manifestations of intelligent performance, a general definition of intelligence should (Legg and Hutter, 2007) : encompass the essence of the different types of intelligence; not be limited to any particular set of senses, environments, goals, and any specific kind of intelligence implementation structures; be based on principles which are fundamental and thus unlikely to alter over time; ideally be formally expressed, objective, and practically realisable as an effective intelligence test.

glossary.definition: Considered in strong connection with human intelligence because intelligent computer systems are designed to support people in completing functions and activities performed by humans in the real world. Historically, it has been established that AI is linked to human intelligence for this reason.
glossary.sentence: However, historically, it has been established that artificial intelligence is considered in a strong connection with human intelligence. This is also justified by a goal defining why intelligent computer systems are developed. People in the real world perform or know how to perform different functions and activities. Intelligent computer systems are designed to support people in the completion of these functions and activities.

glossary.definition: The researcher who offered the first definition of artificial intelligence in 1956. He organized a summer school with scientists interested in automata theory, neural networks, studies of intelligence, and language simulation. The term 'artificial intelligence' was chosen to avoid highlighting a particular topic.
glossary.sentence: The first definition of artificial intelligence was offered by John McCarthy in 1956. At that time, this researcher organized a summer school with the participation of scientists from the United States who were interested in automata theory, neural networks, studies of intelligence and language simulation. The term “artificial intelligence” was chosen as neutral enough to avoid highlighting a particular topic of the summer school.

glossary.definition: Grouped definitions of artificial intelligence into four categories, two of which highlight human intelligence. The categories focus on different aspects (thought processes/reasoning vs behavior) and use two standards: human intelligence or ideal rationality.
glossary.sentence: Meanwhile, Stuart Russell and Peter Norvig (2009) have grouped definitions of artificial intelligence into four categories. Human intelligence is highlighted in two of them. These categories are shown in Figure 2.

glossary.definition: Underlies systems that think humanly by considering humans as information processing systems. Research focuses on how information is represented, processed, transformed, and stored in the human mind and computers.
glossary.sentence: Each category of definitions of artificial intelligence is based on a specific approach used to develop intelligent computer systems (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : A cognitive modelling approach underlies systems that think humanly. It considers human being to be an information processing system. Therefore, research works focus on how information is represented, processed, transformed, and stored in the human mind and in computers.

glossary.definition: An ideal intelligence standard used in some definitions of AI, where knowledge is represented as logical rules to infer new knowledge (e.g., 'All students have a student ID' and 'Alex is a student' implies 'Alex has a student ID').
glossary.sentence: The categories on the right encompass definitions that adopt an ideal intelligence called rationality as a standard (Fig.4). Systems that think rationally are based on logics . As a result, knowledge is represented as logical rules, for example, if we know that “All students have a student ID” and “Alex is a student”, than we can conclude (to infer new knowledge) that “Alex has a student ID”.

glossary.definition: A fundamental test in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence. It involves three rooms: one with an artificial intelligence, another with a human being, and a third with a tester who asks questions without seeing either. If the tester cannot differentiate the answers, the AI is considered intelligent.
glossary.sentence: The Turing test underlies systems that act humanly and it is also one of the fundamental tests in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence.

glossary.definition: systems developed to support humans in completing various functions and activities, or coping with different types of problems.
glossary.sentence: At the beginning of the study course, you learnt out that intelligent computer systems are being developed to support humans in the completion of various functions and activities, or, in other words, to cope with different types of problems.

glossary.definition: Systems acting rationally based on the approach of rational agents.
glossary.sentence: Systems acting rationally are based on the approach of rational agents .

glossary.definition: operates in a changing environment and, to act in the environment, needs to find the best sequence of actions that maximise the end goal.
glossary.sentence: Reinforcement learning is suitable for agents (like robots, self-driving cars, or a game-playing software) who operate in a changing environment and, to act in the environment, they need to find the best sequence of actions that maximise the end goal (Pant, 2019).

glossary.definition: An agent that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.
glossary.sentence: A rational agent is one that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.

glossary.definition: A capability required for a computer system to pass the Turing test, enabling successful communication with the tester as a human being does.
glossary.sentence: To pass the Turing test, the computer system must have the following capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : natural language processing for maintaining successful communication with the tester as the human being does;

glossary.definition: theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems; incorporation of formally described relevant knowledge into an intelligent system to support its performance.
glossary.sentence: In general, “ knowledge representation is the theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems” (Jones, 2009). It can be defined more precisely as an incorporation of full range formally described knowledge into an intelligent system with the aim of supporting intelligent performance of the system (Graham and Barrett, 1997).

glossary.definition: A human capability used to store all previously acquired knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Humans use for this purpose long-term memory to store all previously acquired knowledge and short-term memory - to store knowledge processed at a specific time;

glossary.definition: A human capability used to store knowledge processed at a specific time.
glossary.sentence: Humans use for this purpose long-term memory to store all previously acquired knowledge and short-term memory - to store knowledge processed at a specific time;

glossary.definition: A capability required for a computer system to pass the Turing test, enabling the use of stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions.
glossary.sentence: automated reasoning, in order to use the stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions;

glossary.definition: a method of data analysis that automates analytical model building. It is a branch of artificial intelligence based on the idea that systems can learn from data, identify patterns and make decisions with minimal human intervention.
A version of the Turing test that includes two more requirements: computer vision to perceive objects and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.
glossary.sentence: However, the so called the Total Turing test brings forward two more requirements (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: A requirement for the Total Turing test that enables a computer to perceive objects.
glossary.sentence: the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects,

glossary.definition: A requirement for the Total Turing test that enables a computer to manipulate objects and move about.
glossary.sentence: and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: A scientific field that contributed to artificial intelligence by considering the idea that the human mind is similar to a physical device that operates knowledge, which is encoded using some internal language of the device and can be used to find a correct action.
glossary.sentence: Philosophy - Philosophers made possible artificial intelligence by considering an idea that the human mind is similar to a physical device that operates knowledge. This knowledge is encoded using some internal language of the device and can be used to find a correct action.

glossary.definition: A scientific field that contributed to artificial intelligence by providing mathematical formalization in computing, logics, and probability theory, tools for logical statements and probabilistic judgments, and foundations for reasoning algorithms.
glossary.sentence: Mathematics - The transition from the philosophy to the formal science required mathematical formalization in three main areas: computing, logics, and probability theory. Mathematicians provided tools to work with logical statements and with uncertain, probabilistic judgments. They also laid the foundations for reasoning algorithms.

glossary.definition: A scientific field that contributed to artificial intelligence by offering theories on how to make choices that lead to desired results and laying grounds for studies of rational agents.
glossary.sentence: Economics - It offered theories on how to make choices that lead to the desired results and laid the grounds for studies of rational agents.

glossary.definition: A scientific field that contributed to artificial intelligence by researching how the human brain processes information, leading to the emergence of artificial neural networks which simulate neurons of the human brain.
glossary.sentence: Neuroscience – Contributions of neuroscience in artificial intelligence is related to research on how the human brain processes information. This led to emergence of artificial neural networks which simulate neurons of the human brain and can be used for classification, pattern recognition, optimization, management, and other tasks.

glossary.definition: A scientific field that contributed to artificial intelligence by reinforcing the idea that humans and animals may be seen as information-processing machines, with research questions related to how they think and act.
glossary.sentence: Psychology - Psychologists reinforced the idea that humans and animals may be seen as information-processing machines. The man research questions are related to how humans and animals think and act.

glossary.definition: A scientific field that contributed to artificial intelligence by providing artefacts necessary for developing applications, with progress in computer speed and memory capacity being crucial.
glossary.sentence: Computer engineering – Computer engineering provided artefacts, which made possible development of artificial intelligence applications. Artificial intelligence applications tend to be complex and large, and they could not work without the progress in computer speed and memory capacity, provided by the computer industry. Thus, in this field of science the basic question is how to develop efficient computers.

glossary.definition: Scientific fields that contributed to artificial intelligence with feedback theory, suggesting that intelligent behaviour results from feedback mechanisms which can be simulated by computers. The main research question is how intelligent artefacts can operate under their own control.
glossary.sentence: Control science and cybernetics - The main contribution of this field to artificial intelligence is related to the feedback theory, which offered an idea that all intelligent behaviour is the result of feedback mechanism, that is, a mechanism which can be simulated also by the computer. The main research question is how intelligent artefacts can operate under their own control.

glossary.definition: A scientific field that contributed to artificial intelligence by showing that language use fits the model of humans and animals as data processing machines, contributing to natural language processing and knowledge representation.
glossary.sentence: Linguistics - Linguists showed that the use of language fits the model of psychologists that humans and animals can be considered as data processing machines. Studies of how language is related to thinking contributed to natural language processing and knowledge representation in the field of artificial intelligence.

glossary.definition: The goal of artificial intelligence related to understanding principles that make possible intelligent performance in both natural and artificial systems, involving analysis of manifestations, hypothesis formulation, testing, and experimentation with computational systems.
glossary.sentence: Goals of artificial intelligence can be considered along two dimensions (Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : scientific goal is related to understanding of those principles which make possible intelligent performance both in natural and artificial systems. To achieve this goal, it is necessary to analyse manifestations of intelligent performance in different types of systems, to formulate and test hypotheses about what it takes to construct intelligent artefacts, as well as designing, building and experimenting with computational systems that are able of demonstrating intelligent performance;

glossary.definition: The goal of artificial intelligence related to the development of useful intelligent artefacts.
glossary.sentence: engineering goal is related to the development of useful intelligent artefacts.

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems
glossary.sentence: Connectionist artificial intelligence Symbolic artificial intelligence An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons) An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons)
glossary.sentence: Connectionist artificial intelligence Symbolic artificial intelligence An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons) An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems

glossary.definition: defined by Newell and Simon in 1976. It defines how the general intelligent performance of both humans and computers is achieved and is formulated as follows: “A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action” (Newell and Simon, 1976).
glossary.sentence: Symbolic artificial intelligence is based on the physical symbol system hypothesis , defined by Newell and Simon in 1976. It defines how the general intelligent performance of both humans and computers is achieved and is formulated as follows: “A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action” (Newell and Simon, 1976).

glossary.definition: correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality.
glossary.sentence: A physical symbol system includes (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009; Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality.

glossary.definition: consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way. Symbolic structures thus depict important aspects of a problem domain; operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.
glossary.sentence: A physical symbol system includes (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009; Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : ... symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way. Symbolic structures thus depict important aspects of a problem domain; operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.

glossary.definition: (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.
glossary.sentence: A physical symbol system includes (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009; Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : ... operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.

glossary.definition: essentially involves transforming physical symbols according to rules.
glossary.sentence: Summarising all of the above, it can be said that " all intelligent behavior essentially involves transforming physical symbols according to rules " (Bermúdez, 2014).

glossary.definition: (objects and their relations) through a set of symbols.
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems based on this paradigm therefore explicitly represent problem domain knowledge (objects and their relations) through a set of symbols (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: a physical system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols.
glossary.sentence: A physical system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: uses symbols 0 and 1. These symbols are combined into strings of 0 and 1 to represent objects in the world, e.g. the digit "3" is represented by the string "00110011". These strings are symbolic structures. The computer creates these structures and operates on them using basic machine language routines.
glossary.sentence: Consider the example of a computer (Fig. The computer uses symbols 0 and 1. These symbols are combined into strings of 0 and 1 to represent objects in the world, e.g. the digit "3" is represented by the string "00110011". These strings are symbolic structures. The computer creates these structures and operates on them using basic machine language routines.

glossary.definition: describes a situation in which the physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols, but the system has no intelligence.
glossary.sentence: John Searle’s Chinese room argument is a famous example of criticism. Searle describes a situation in which the physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols, but the system has no intelligence.

glossary.definition: simplified models of the human brain consisting of a large number of connected processing units (artificial neurons) that mimic the neurons of the human brain, and numerical weights that are attached to the connections between the units, reflecting the strength of these connections.
glossary.sentence: This approach has emerged from attempts to mathematically model neural activity in the human brain and is based on so-called artificial neural networks. Artificial neural networks are simplified models of the human brain consisting of a large number of connected processing units (artificial neurons) that mimic the neurons of the human brain, and numerical weights that are attached to the connections between the units, reflecting the strength of these connections.

glossary.definition: is not directly represented in the system because it is considered as a distributed quantity in a connected group of smaller processing units, rather than a quantity encoded in physical symbols.
glossary.sentence: In connectionist artificial intelligence, there is an opposite view of the concept of knowledge: knowledge is not directly represented in the system because it is considered as a distributed quantity in a connected group of smaller processing units, rather than a quantity encoded in physical symbols (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: (artificial neural networks) that mimic the neurons of the human brain.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks are simplified models of the human brain consisting of a large number of connected processing units (artificial neurons) that mimic the neurons of the human brain, and numerical weights that are attached to the connections between the units, reflecting the strength of these connections.

glossary.definition: Values that the perceptron learns to allow correct classification of data.
glossary.sentence: The perceptron learns the best possible values for weights that will allow correctly to classify the data (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: designed to play the board game Go.
glossary.sentence: for example, tree-based search complements artificial neural networks in the AlphaGo program, which is designed to play the board game Go (Goel, 2021).

glossary.definition: Proves to be learnable
glossary.sentence: Connectionist representation of knowledge proves to be learnable Symbolic representation of knowledge is not much learnable Connectionist artificial intelligence solves the problem but is not able to effectively reason the solution

glossary.definition: Is not much learnable
glossary.sentence: Connectionist representation of knowledge proves to be learnable Symbolic representation of knowledge is not much learnable Connectionist artificial intelligence solves the problem but is not able to effectively reason the solution

glossary.definition: Also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc.
glossary.sentence: There are several approaches to artificial intelligence , based on comparing the capabilities of artificial intelligence systems with those of humans. Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence , that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc. ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) . This is the only approach in which significant progress has been achieved so far.

glossary.definition: Also called strong artificial intelligence or human-level intelligence, treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated. Related to development of computer systems capable of solving wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions
glossary.sentence: Artificial general intelligence or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) . It is related to the development of such computer systems that are capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) .

glossary.definition: Intelligence that surpasses human capabilities, much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills
glossary.sentence: Artificial super intelligence is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015) .

glossary.definition: Focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales. Asserts that true intelligence involves the ability to function in a real-world environment. Based on hypothesis that intelligent systems need representations grounded in physical world with sensors and actuators
glossary.sentence: Additionally, to the previously mentioned approaches there is also the so called new/nouvelle artificial intelligence . “It focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales” ( Floreano and Mattiussi, 2008). Practitioners of new artificial intelligence assert that true intelligence involves the ability to function in a real-world environment (Copeland, 2018; Pfeifer and Scheier, 2001) . Brooks (1990) , the founder of this approach, has emphasised that the new artificial intelligence is based on the hypothesis that in order to build an intelligent system, it is necessary that their representations are grounded in the physical world, since the world is the best model of itself; in this case, of course, it is necessary to equip the system with sensors in order for the system to perceive the environment and actuators in order for the system to be able to operate in the environment.

glossary.definition: Paradigm under new artificial intelligence that considers collective intelligence as behaviour emerging through interaction of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents. Includes ant colony optimization and particle swarm optimization, used in search and optimisation
glossary.sentence: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents. It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) . These two sub-directions are used in search and optimisation.

glossary.definition: Studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by ant behaviour when searching for food
glossary.sentence: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents. It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) . These two sub-directions are used in search and optimisation.

glossary.definition: Studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish
glossary.sentence: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents. It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) . These two sub-directions are used in search and optimisation.

glossary.definition: Use general methods like general purpose search, logics, syntax-based calculations, automatic reasoning that could be applied to broad set of problems without using knowledge on problem under consideration
glossary.sentence: Methods of artificial intelligence can be divided into two groups which could not be confused with the approaches of artificial intelligence considered before (Russell and Norvig, 2020; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011; Brownlee, 2011) : weak methods use general methods (like general purpose search, logics, syntax-based calculations, automatic reasoning, etc.) which could be applied to a broad set of problems. They do not use knowledge on a problem under consideration;

glossary.definition: Based on great amount of knowledge about problem under consideration and take into account problem solving context; typically use weak methods
glossary.sentence: Methods of artificial intelligence can be divided into two groups which could not be confused with the approaches of artificial intelligence considered before (Russell and Norvig, 2020; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011; Brownlee, 2011) : weak methods use general methods (like general purpose search, logics, syntax-based calculations, automatic reasoning, etc.) which could be applied to a broad set of problems. They do not use knowledge on a problem under consideration; strong methods are based on the great amount of knowledge about a problem under consideration and, thus, they take into account problem solving context; strong methods typically use weak methods.

glossary.definition: a way of structural organization of data about the real world.
glossary.sentence: we need to think about: data structures or, in other words, a way of structural organization of data about the real world;

glossary.definition: places where to store the data, for example, files, databases.
glossary.sentence: we need to think about: ...; data repositories or places where to store the data, for example, files, databases,

glossary.definition: ways to process data in order to achieve a specific goal.
glossary.sentence: we need to think about: ...; algorithms or ways to process data in order to achieve a specific goal.

glossary.definition: a mathematical representation that uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.
glossary.sentence: The formal representation uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.

glossary.definition: a process where humans first need to become aware that the problem exists, define it by determining an initial state (current state), a goal state (desired state), and check for discrepancy between them.
glossary.sentence: To talk about the need to solve a problem, humans first need to become aware that the problem exists or, in other words, the problem needs to be defined. The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process), a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process) and whether there is a discrepancy between these two states.

glossary.definition: the current state at the beginning of the problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process)

glossary.definition: the desired state to be reached at the end of the problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine ... a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process)

glossary.definition: a difference between the initial and goal states, indicating that there is a problem.
glossary.sentence: ...and whether there is a discrepancy between these two states (Figure 4). Let's consider an example... Since you are still in your apartment, there is a discrepancy between the initial and goal states of the problem-solving process, which means that you have a problem.

glossary.definition: aspects evaluated to identify possible actions for solving a problem (e.g., available transport options, time, money, traffic conditions).
glossary.sentence: Once we have defined the problem, we can start thinking about resources and opportunities available to us, which together allow the identification of actions that we can take to solve the problem.

glossary.definition: possible solutions identified based on evaluating available resources and opportunities.
glossary.sentence: All of these aspects allow you to identify possible alternative actions, what kind of transport vehicles to use and under what conditions (Figure 6).

glossary.definition: a set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at the specified time.
glossary.sentence: A state is a set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at the specified time.

glossary.definition: The representation of all possible game states and transitions between them.
glossary.sentence: Although we have applied the minimax algorithm and alpha-beta pruning to a complete state space graph in this game, these principles can also be used in strategies for processing a partial state space graph.

glossary.definition: a node in the state space graph corresponding to an initial state; structurally, it has no ancestors.
glossary.sentence: The structural viewpoint includes: nodes corresponding to discrete states... a state space graph should have at least one root node that corresponds to an initial state. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that do not have ancestors;

glossary.definition: a node in the state space graph corresponding to a goal state; structurally, it has no descendants.
glossary.sentence: The structural viewpoint includes: ...; a state space graph should have at least one terminal (leaf) node, which corresponds to a goal state. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that do not have descendants;

glossary.definition: nodes in the state space graph corresponding to intermediate states; structurally, they have both ancestors and descendants.
glossary.sentence: The structural viewpoint includes: ...; a state space graph may have internal nodes that correspond to intermediate states. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that have both ancestors and descendants.

glossary.definition: directed lines in the state space graph representing transitions between states or executable actions in a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: The structural viewpoint includes: ...; arcs that represent transitions between states or, in other words, executable actions in a problem-solving process. Arcs in the state space graph should be directed to show the changing of states in a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: a way of organizing and storing data about states and actions of the problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: It is a data structure organizing and storing data about states and actions of the problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: a system that needs a search algorithm to find a solution based on known input data.
glossary.sentence: Additionally, an intelligent computer system needs a search algorithm that processes the data structure to find a solution to the problem based on known input data (Figure 1).

glossary.definition: A process that sequentially explores different paths in a state space graph to find a solution path, potentially facing situations that affect its efficiency and success.
glossary.sentence: The search principles explained previously have shown that while searching for a solution path, the search algorithm sequentially explores different paths in the state space graph. This process may result in several situations that determine how fast and whether the search algorithm will find a solution path.

glossary.definition: The desired route through a state space graph that leads to the goal node.
glossary.sentence: Let’s look at these situations. Imagine that the search algorithm is searching for a solution path in the state space graph you see in Figure 15.

glossary.definition: initial node added to OPEN list at iteration 0; first current node in algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 0: The search start node is added to the OPEN list together with its heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: node that the algorithm aims to reach; triggers stopping when added to OPEN list or when leaf nodes with better heuristic measures are found.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm stops when the search goal node is added to the OPEN list or when leaf nodes with heuristic measures better than the heuristic measure of the search goal node are reached.

glossary.definition: a sequence corresponding to a solution path, leading to the solution to the problem.
glossary.sentence: It corresponds to an ordered sequence of actions that lead to the solution to the problem.

glossary.definition: represent actions of the problem-solving process in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: taking into account that graph arcs represent actions of the problem-solving process,

glossary.definition: a sequence of movements derived from a solution path (e.g., left-right-straight ahead).
glossary.sentence: we get a robot motion trajectory - the robot should move left-right-straight ahead.

glossary.definition: node taken from first position of OPEN list at each iteration for expansion.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm takes the node located on the first position in the OPEN list—the node A—and it becomes the current node.

glossary.definition: nodes that have been generated but whose descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated.
glossary.sentence: As a result of this action, two types of nodes appear: open nodes – the search algorithm has already generated these nodes, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated;

glossary.definition: nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.
glossary.sentence: As a result of this action, two types of nodes appear: open nodes – the search algorithm has already generated these nodes, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated; closed nodes – they are nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.

glossary.definition: a search method where the system starts by applying known facts.
glossary.sentence: the intelligent computer system can start by applying the data-driven search and demand the user to deliver known facts

glossary.definition: a technique for gathering information and inferring from it whatever can be inferred, typically used for problems of interpretation where most data are initially given.
glossary.sentence: Forward chaining is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred ( Negnevitsky , 2011). It is typically used for problems of interpretation because, in this type of problems, most of the data are initially given, and the system searches for their interpretation (Luger, 2009; Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: a search method that switches to inferring from known facts when new information is available.
glossary.sentence: the system can switch on to the goal-driven search, as well as to switch on back to the data-driven search when new facts are entering the system intending to maximise the use of new information in the problem-solving process

glossary.definition: a technique where the expert system has a goal and attempts to find evidence to prove it, corresponding roughly to hypothesis testing in human problem solving.
glossary.sentence: Backward chaining corresponds to a decomposition of the problem’s goal into simpler subgoals; thus, backward search in an expert system corresponds roughly to the process of hypothesis testing in human problem solving (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: nodes representing initial states of the problem-solving process in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: If it is data-driven search, then the search start node is selected from the set of root nodes,

glossary.definition: nodes representing goal states of the problem-solving process in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: If it is data-driven search, then the search start node is selected from the set of root nodes, but the search goal node– from the set of leaf nodes.

glossary.definition: Paths in a state space graph that can vary in number depending on direction.
glossary.sentence: For example, if the state space graph represents a maze and it is necessary to find an exit, then it would be wise to start with the exit, because the number of routes leading from the exit to the entrance is smaller than the number of routes leading from the entrance to the exit (Figure 12).

glossary.definition: Information that must be acquired by an algorithm as it is not initially provided.
glossary.sentence: Problem data are not given but must be acquired by the algorithm.

glossary.definition: A process where a doctor develops hypotheses about potential diagnoses based on patient symptoms and orders necessary tests to confirm or deny them (Figure 13).
glossary.sentence: In medical diagnosis, after the patient describes his/her symptoms, the doctor develops a hypothesis about the potential diagnosis. Taking into account that there is a variety of diagnostic tests that can be applied, the doctor orders only those that are necessary to confirm or deny a hypothesis about the patient's diagnosis (Figure 13).

glossary.definition: Tests that can be applied in medical diagnosis to confirm or deny hypotheses.
glossary.sentence: Taking into account that there is a variety of diagnostic tests that can be applied, the doctor orders only those that are necessary to confirm or deny a hypothesis about the patient's diagnosis (Figure 13).

glossary.definition: A potential diagnosis developed by a doctor after hearing a patient's symptoms.
glossary.sentence: In medical diagnosis, after the patient describes his/her symptoms, the doctor develops a hypothesis about the potential diagnosis.

glossary.definition: Results produced when rules match problem data; their number increases as more rules are applied.
glossary.sentence: There are a large number of rules that match the data of the problem and thus produce an increasing number of conclusions or goals.

glossary.definition: The choice of which way to search in a state space graph, determined by factors like data availability, goal definition ease, and graph structure.
glossary.sentence: To select a search direction, it is necessary to analyse a problem by taking the following factors into account: availability of data about the problem; easiness of defining a search goal; the structure of the state space graph.

glossary.definition: Nodes reachable from a current node along a path; their examination may help avoid redundant searches (Figure 15).
glossary.sentence: The search algorithm will first generate the path A->B->F->J. It will determine that this path does not lead to the search goal node, and the search algorithm will continue expanding nodes and will generate the path A->B->G->K.

glossary.definition: Resources like search time and computer memory that can be saved by excluding already expanded nodes from further searching.
glossary.sentence: So it would be logical to exclude this node from the further searching process to save computational resources: search time and computer memory.

glossary.definition: A loop in a state space graph where a node is revisited, potentially causing the search algorithm to get stuck (e.g., nodes H-D-C in Figure 15).
glossary.sentence: As this path also does not lead to the search goal node, the search algorithm will start generating the path A->C->G. When reaching the node G, we, people, can see that this node was already expanded by generating the previous path... In this case, the search algorithm can get stuck in the cycle and never find the search goal node.

glossary.definition: A meta-strategy for systematically trying different paths through a state space graph; it answers questions about repeatedly discovered nodes and cycles by backtracking to unexplored branches (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: Backtracking gives answers to the questions about the repeatedly discovered nodes and cycles. The principle of selecting the next current node is determined by a specific search algorithm (Figure 16). Backtracking is nowadays considered to be a meta-strategy for systematically trying different paths through a state space graph, because its ideas are used by other search algorithms that are able to find a solution path in a more effective way than Backtracking.

glossary.definition: A higher-level strategy (like backtracking) whose principles are applied by other algorithms for more efficient searching.
glossary.sentence: Backtracking is nowadays considered to be a meta-strategy for systematically trying different paths through a state space graph, because its ideas are used by other search algorithms that are able to find a solution path in a more effective way than Backtracking.

glossary.definition: A node in the state space graph with no children, representing a terminal state of the game.
glossary.sentence: until it reaches a leaf node or a node with a precise heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: algorithms that receive the start node, goal node, and state space graph as inputs; their main steps include selecting current node, checking if it is the goal, and expanding it.
glossary.sentence: They receive the search start node and the search goal node as input data, the state space graph, a data structure, is used to search for a problem solution, and they provide a solution path as their output. They have three main operational steps (Figure 1): selecting the current node; checking if the current node is the search goal node; expanding the current node if it is not the search goal node.

glossary.definition: algorithms that use an additional input—heuristic evaluation function—and include steps to evaluate and order nodes by heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic search algorithms differ both in the inputs that they need and the operational steps they perform (Figure 2). Heuristic search algorithms receive an additional input - the principles of calculation of the heuristic measures of nodes or, in other words, a heuristic evaluation function. Moreover, heuristic search algorithms have two additional steps - after expanding the current node, they evaluate the generated ancestors/descendants (depending on the search direction) by applying a heuristic evaluation function and then select the best node and/or order the nodes according to heuristic measures.

glossary.definition: The measure of whether a node leads to the search goal and how quickly, used in heuristic searches.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic search algorithms use not only the state space graph but also additional information about node quality. The quality is measured by considering if a specific node leads to the search goal node and how quickly it leads to the search goal node.

glossary.definition: a system of roads between cities (e.g., Paris and Toulouse) used to demonstrate differences between search algorithms.
glossary.sentence: Imagine that we are building a recommendation system that will advise tourists about the selection of roads between two cities in France: Paris and Toulouse.

glossary.definition: a system that advises tourists on road selection between cities.
glossary.sentence: Imagine that we are building a recommendation system that will advise tourists about the selection of roads between two cities in France: Paris and Toulouse.

glossary.definition: algorithms that process only nodes and arcs of the state space graph without additional information about node quality.
glossary.sentence: In the case of uninformed search, all of the paths represented in the state space graph are identical, because uninformed search algorithms process only nodes and arcs of the state space graph. They do not have additional information about the quality of the nodes.

glossary.definition: algorithms that distinguish between paths by using additional information like distance, location, road surface, traffic lights, etc.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic search algorithms are able to distinguish between paths represented in the state space graph. Therefore, each path differs from all of the others. This is because heuristic search can take into account such information as the distance between any two settlements, the location of each settlement (north, west, etc.) relative to Paris, the surface of the road leading to each settlement, the number of traffic lights between any two settlements, and many other factors.

glossary.definition: algorithms that find a solution path by performing steps like selecting current node, checking if it is goal node, and expanding nodes.
glossary.sentence: In the previous topic of the course, you have already considered that any search algorithm performs three main steps: selecting the current node; checking if the current node is the search goal node; expanding the current node.

glossary.definition: list that contains nodes to be expanded; always contains the same number of nodes as the beam width, excluding iteration 0 when only the search start node is added.
glossary.sentence: The OPEN list always contains the same number of nodes as the beam width, excluding: iteration 0, when only the search start node is added to the OPEN list, and a case when the number of direct descendants/ancestors is less than the beam width.

glossary.definition: list where other nodes (not chosen as best) are placed after being expanded or generated.
glossary.sentence: Other nodes are placed on the CLOSED list.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm searches the state space graph using a depth-first search (Ertel, 2011),

glossary.definition: a data structure where the last element added is the first one processed (LIFO principle).
glossary.sentence: In the implementation of this algorithm, new nodes are added to the left side of the OPEN list. Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a stack.

glossary.definition: the principle where the last element entered is the first one processed (e.g., in a stack).
glossary.sentence: A stack works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: The current node being expanded in an iteration.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 5: The algorithm expands the current node I by generating its three descendants - nodes J, K, and G.

glossary.definition: Three descendants generated from the expansion of node I.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 5: The algorithm expands the current node I by generating its three descendants - nodes J, K, and G.

glossary.definition: The minimum number of steps or edges required to reach the search goal node from the start node.
glossary.sentence: However, this algorithm is not guaranteed to find the shortest path to the search goal node, if such a path exists in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: Paths that extend without bound in depth, causing the algorithm to take excessive time searching.
glossary.sentence: The reason is that the algorithm always goes deeper into the state space graph and finds the first path to the search goal node, which may not be the shortest one. Besides that, the algorithm has another serious shortcoming—the problem of so-called indefinitely deep paths.

glossary.definition: A modified version of Depth-First Search that restricts the depth to which the search can proceed, defined by a specified limit.
glossary.sentence: To overcome this shortcoming, a modification of Depth-First Search is offered. Depth-Limited Search is a modification of Depth-First Search that overcomes a drawback of Depth-First Search related to going along indefinitely deep paths.

glossary.definition: A predefined maximum depth that the algorithm can explore in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The title of the algorithm reveals its main idea (Figure 10): “depth” means to go deeper and deeper into the state space graph while expanding nodes; “limited” indicates that there is a depth limit that defines how deeply the state space graph can be explored.

glossary.definition: Nodes at the specified depth limit, considered as terminal points for expansion.
glossary.sentence: Nodes located at the depth limit are considered to be leaf/root nodes (depending on the search direction).

glossary.definition: Labels assigned to nodes indicating their level in the state space graph, used to enforce the depth limit.
glossary.sentence: In the implementation of the algorithm, the depth limit should be defined, and nodes should be added to both lists together with their depth labels.

glossary.definition: A numerical value representing how far a node is from the search start node in terms of steps taken.
glossary.sentence: The depth level of any other node is calculated by adding 1 to the depth level of the current node.

glossary.definition: A descendant generated from expanding the search start node A, located at depth 1 in an example of Depth-Limited Search.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 1: The algorithm expands the current node A by generating its three descendants - nodes D, H, and E. None of the generated nodes have been added to the OPEN or CLOSED lists. Thus, they all are added to the OPEN list in the sequence that they were found.

glossary.definition: A descendant node generated from expanding node D at depth 2 in an example of Depth-Limited Search.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 2: The algorithm expands the current node D by generating its descendant - the node J. Taking into account that the node J has not been added to the OPEN or CLOSED lists, it is added to the left side of the OPEN list.

glossary.definition: A descendant generated from expanding the search start node A at depth 1 in an example of Depth-Limited Search.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 3: The algorithm moves the node J to the CLOSED list. The node H, which is located on the first position of the OPEN list, becomes the current node.

glossary.definition: A descendant generated from expanding the search start node A at depth 1 in an example of Depth-Limited Search.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 4: The algorithm expands the current node H by generating its three descendants - nodes J, I, and E. Taking into account that the node J has been already added to the CLOSED list and that the node E has been already added to the OPEN list, they both are not added to the OPEN list.

glossary.definition: The search goal node in an example of Depth-Limited Search.
glossary.sentence: Let us consider an example of Depth-Limited Search by implementing data-driven search in the state space graph you see in Figure 13. The search start node is A. The search goal node is K.

glossary.definition: A descendant generated from expanding node E at depth 2 in an example of Depth-Limited Search.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 6: The algorithm expands the current node E by generating its three descendants - nodes I, K, and F. Taking into account that the node I is already added to the CLOSED list, it is not added to the OPEN list again.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that explores closer branches and then goes to the ends of branches.
glossary.sentence: The difference between Breadth-First Search and Bidirectional Search is evident if we use the analogy of the state space graph and tree crown (Figure 21). Breadth-First Search explores closer branches and then goes to the ends of branches.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that performs a search simultaneously in both directions - data-driven search and goal-driven search - until it finds a point of contact in both directions.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm performs a search simultaneously in both directions - data-driven search and goal-driven search - until it finds a point of contact in both directions.

glossary.definition: Lists used in Bidirectional Search to store nodes that need to be expanded.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm uses two OPEN lists and two CLOSED lists. The lists are filled in simultaneously in both directions.

glossary.definition: Lists used in Bidirectional Search to store nodes that have already been expanded.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm uses two OPEN lists and two CLOSED lists. The lists are filled in simultaneously in both directions.

glossary.definition: The initial step in Bidirectional Search where the search start node is added to the OPEN list of data-driven search and the search goal node is added to the OPEN list of goal-driven search.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 0: The search start node - the node A - is added to the OPEN list of data-driven search. The search goal node - the node K - is added to the OPEN list of goal-driven search.

glossary.definition: The step in Bidirectional Search where nodes A and K are expanded simultaneously, and their descendants or ancestors are added to the OPEN lists.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 1: The algorithm expands the node A and the node K simultaneously. The descendants of the node A are the nodes D, H, and E. None of them have been added to the OPEN list or to the CLOSED list of data-driven search. Thus, all three nodes are added to the OPEN list in the sequence that they were found.

glossary.definition: knowledge applied in intelligent computer systems to reduce complexity.
glossary.sentence: I hope that you now know a lot about heuristic knowledge and its application in intelligent computer systems,

glossary.definition: Strategies used by people to find a path from the initial state to the goal state effectively.
glossary.sentence: Taking into account that a problem can have a large number of solutions, people use strategies (heuristic methods) to find a path from the initial state to the goal state in an effective way (Eysenck & Keane, 2000).

glossary.definition: A widely used concept corresponding to heuristic knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Today, the widely used concept of “life hack” corresponds to what is understood by heuristic knowledge.

glossary.definition: a function used to find the most promising moves in game strategies. It is applied with limited look-ahead and may not detect a heuristically promising path, leading to potential bad situations later in the game.
glossary.sentence: They can be based on the principles of either the Minimax or Alpha-beta pruning, and use a heuristic evaluation function to find the most promising moves.

glossary.definition: The amount of mental effort required for in-depth analysis of a problem.
glossary.sentence: It reduces the cognitive workload that would be needed for the in-depth analysis of a problem

glossary.definition: The number of points explored by an intelligent computer system during search.
glossary.sentence: The function reduces the number of nodes visited by an intelligent computer system during search and the search time

glossary.definition: The duration taken to find a solution in a search process.
glossary.sentence: The function reduces the number of nodes visited by an intelligent computer system during search and the search time

glossary.definition: values assigned to the nodes of the available graph that allow the intelligent computer system to select promising moves during game play.
glossary.sentence: In both cases, the intelligent computer system assigns heuristic measures to the nodes of the available graph, and these allow the intelligent computer system to select promising moves to make during game play.

glossary.definition: The distance between the search start node and node n in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration; g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n;

glossary.definition: A heuristic measure that estimates the distance between node n and the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration; ... h(n) – a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node;

glossary.definition: A form of heuristic evaluation function used by Samuel in his checker program.
glossary.sentence: As a result, the heuristic evaluation function can be represented as a polynomial function, as used by Samuel in his checker program ((Samuel, 1959) as cited in (Luger, 2009)):

glossary.definition: Factors that characterize states in the problem-solving process and their importance for reaching the goal state.
glossary.sentence: In practical applications, the previously mentioned elements of the heuristic evaluation function are represented indirectly by focusing on factors characterizing states in the problem-solving process and their importance for reaching the goal state (Figure 3).

glossary.definition: A game used as an example to demonstrate the heuristic evaluation function, where the system evaluates moves based on potentially winning positions.
glossary.sentence: Imagine we are building an intelligent computer system that will play a game of Tic-tac-toe.

glossary.definition: A puzzle with 8 numbered tiles and 9 squares, used as an example to demonstrate the heuristic evaluation function based on the number of tiles out of place.
glossary.sentence: Another example is the game of 8-puzzle. There are 8 numbered tiles and 9 squares.

glossary.definition: a characteristic used to evaluate nodes in a state space graph, such as the number of tiles out of place, sum of distances of tiles from their intended places, or number of tile reversals.
glossary.sentence: Applying the defined factor, the first and the third node will acquire a value of 5, while the second node will get a value of 3, and therefore, this could be considered a promising move.

glossary.definition: a point in the state space graph that represents a particular state or configuration.
glossary.sentence: Applying the defined factor, the first and the third node will acquire a value of 5, while the second node will get a value of 3,

glossary.definition: the number of tiles that are not in their intended positions on the board.
glossary.sentence: f(n) = the number of tiles out of place

glossary.definition: the total distance of all tiles from their intended places on the board.
glossary.sentence: Another factor that can be used to evaluate nodes is to sum up distances of tiles from their intended places (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: pairs of tiles located close to each other that need to be swapped, which usually takes more than two moves to correct.
glossary.sentence: We can define one more factor for this game: the number of pairs of tile reversals (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: the average number of arcs per node in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The complexity of a state space graph depends on two factors: B – the branching factor of the state space graph. It is the average number of arcs per node.

glossary.definition: the length of the longest path in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: L – depth of the state space graph (usually the length of the longest path).

glossary.definition: the total count of nodes in a state space graph, which can be estimated based on branching factor and depth.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the total number of nodes (T) is

glossary.definition: a problem that occurs when a heuristic evaluation function is applied with limited look-ahead, leading to undetected heuristically promising paths and potential loss of the game.
glossary.sentence: One of the most significant problems with this approach is called the horizon effect (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009). When a heuristic evaluation function is applied with limited look-ahead, it is possible that the depth of the look-ahead may not detect a heuristically promising path, and this may lead to a bad situation later in the game.

glossary.definition: a state space graph that cannot be fully stored or processed due to limited computational resources, only a part of it is generated as needed.
glossary.sentence: Only a part of a state space graph (a partly available state space graph). It is not possible to store and process the whole graph due to limited computational resources.

glossary.definition: a state space graph that can be fully stored and processed because sufficient computational resources are available.
glossary.sentence: A full state space graph (a fully available state space graph). Computational resources are enough to store and process such a graph.

glossary.definition: a parameter that when doubled from 6 to 12, increases the number of nodes to visit exponentially (from 819 to 8190).
glossary.sentence: However, by doubling the depth (12), the number of nodes to visit is 10 times larger (8190 nodes). So, there is exponential growth.

glossary.term: transformation of a state space graph
glossary.definition: a method to reduce the number of states in a game tree, though there are no clear guidelines on how to perform this transformation.
glossary.sentence: One of the methods is to transform a state space graph before its use by the intelligent computer system. Unfortunately, there are no clear guidelines on how to make such a transformation.

glossary.definition: a characteristic that allows reducing the number of states in a game tree by considering equivalent positions, as demonstrated in Tic-tac-toe.
glossary.sentence: One of the suggestions is to use a symmetry feature of states, which can be done for the game of Tic-tac-toe (Figure 12).

glossary.definition: a structure representing possible moves in a game; its complexity can be reduced by considering symmetric positions.
glossary.sentence: At the first level of the game tree, it is possible to consider only three states: corner, centre, and side, because, for example, it does not matter in which corner the sign is placed.

glossary.definition: a heuristic search algorithm that moves to the best node at each step, without backtracking; stops when the goal is reached or a local optimum is found.
glossary.sentence: The idea of the Hill Climbing algorithm is to always move to the best node in the state space graph. [...] The algorithm does not implement backtracking. The search stops when: the search goal node is reached or when a node whose heuristic measure is better than the measures of its descendants/ancestors is found.

glossary.definition: a measure acquired for terminal nodes using a heuristic evaluation function and propagated back up to the current state node based on principles like minimax or alpha-beta pruning.
glossary.sentence: It applies a heuristic evaluation function and acquires heuristic measures for the terminal nodes. Following this, the heuristic measures are propagated back up to the node of the current state, based on the principles of the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: simultaneously expands all nodes located in the OPEN list at each iteration, evaluates their descendants/ancestors (depending on a search direction), and chooses the best nodes from the generated nodes according to the beam width.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm always simultaneously expands all nodes located in the OPEN list at each iteration, evaluates their descendants/ancestors (depending on a search direction), and chooses the best nodes from the generated nodes according to the beam width.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that always selects the best node based on heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: It should be noted that Hill Climbing always selects the best node.

glossary.definition: The node with the most favorable heuristic measure as determined by the algorithm's function.
glossary.sentence: Hill Climbing always selects the best node. The best node is determined by the heuristic measures of nodes.

glossary.definition: A shortcoming where Hill Climbing gets stuck at a node with a better heuristic measure than its descendants/ancestors, preventing it from reaching the search goal.
glossary.sentence: the local maximum problem : the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction )

glossary.definition: A shortcoming where Hill Climbing cannot continue searching if the best descendant/ancestor's heuristic measure equals that of the current node.
glossary.sentence: the plateau problem : the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that selects the best node found so far in each iteration and keeps all previously found nodes, avoiding some shortcomings of Hill Climbing.
glossary.sentence: The Best-First Search algorithm does not have these shortcomings.

glossary.definition: A data structure used to implement the OPEN list, where the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.
glossary.sentence: The OPEN list is implemented as a data structure called a priority queue (Figure 7). The priority queue works according to the principle that the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.

glossary.definition: nodes generated by expanding a current node; direction depends on search orientation.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm always simultaneously expands all nodes located in the OPEN list at each iteration, evaluates their descendants/ancestors (depending on a search direction)...

glossary.definition: algorithm that expands all OPEN list nodes simultaneously, selects best nodes based on beam width, and uses heuristic evaluation without backtracking.
glossary.sentence: Let us consider an example of Beam Search by implementing data-driven search on the state space graph...

glossary.definition: number of best nodes selected at each iteration based on heuristic measures; determines size of OPEN list (except for specific cases).
glossary.sentence: The algorithm always simultaneously expands all nodes located in the OPEN list at each iteration, evaluates their descendants/ancestors (depending on a search direction), and chooses the best nodes from the generated nodes according to the beam width.

glossary.definition: a graph where we can use specific values for heuristic measures depending on the number of possible game results.
glossary.sentence: In the case of the complete state space graph, we can use the following values in the simplest case.

glossary.definition: a more complex case since the results of the game cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.
glossary.sentence: A partial state space graph is a more complex case, since the results of the game cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.

glossary.definition: The player whose objective is to maximize the game result (win).
glossary.sentence: the task of the maximiser is to win the game.

glossary.definition: The player whose objective is to minimize the game result for the maximiser (prevent winning).
glossary.sentence: the task of the minimiser is to minimise the game result for the maximiser, i.e. to prevent the maximiser from winning the game.

glossary.definition: A decision-making layer where the maximiser is choosing, aiming to maximize the game result (win).
glossary.sentence: The current node is located at the MAX level and the only known heuristic measure is 1.

glossary.definition: A decision-making layer where the minimiser is choosing, aiming to minimize the game result for the maximiser.
glossary.sentence: The current node is located at the MIN level, but there is no difference between the heuristic measures of the descendants.

glossary.definition: the heuristic measure assigned to the root node in the case of a complete state space graph, which shows the result of the game if both players play rationally.
glossary.sentence: In the case of a complete state space graph, a heuristic measure assigned to the root node is called a game result . It clearly shows the result of the game if both players play rationally, without making mistakes, and with the aim of winning the game.

glossary.definition: paths that start at the root node and reach a leaf node while satisfying conditions where all nodes in the path are closely related and have the same heuristic measures equal to the game result.
glossary.sentence: A winning path is a path that starts at the root node and reaches a leaf node while satisfying the following conditions: all nodes in the winning path are closely related; all nodes in the winning path have the same heuristic measures, which are equal to the game result.

glossary.definition: A decision-making algorithm for minimizing the possible loss for a worst-case scenario.
glossary.sentence: This is a game result, and we can see that it is the same as provided by the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: a principle used in game strategies where heuristic measures are propagated back up to the current node using its rules.
glossary.sentence: The two approaches of N-ply look-ahead and iterative deepening are used to process a partial state space graph (Figure 11). They can be based on the principles of either the Minimax or Alpha-beta pruning, and use a heuristic evaluation function to find the most promising moves.

glossary.definition: a value assigned to nodes at MAX levels that should not decrease as it defines the best move to maximize the game result.
glossary.sentence: An alpha number (α) is assigned to nodes at MAX levels. This value should not decrease, since this would represent a bad move.

glossary.definition: a value assigned to nodes at MIN levels that should not increase as it defines the best move to minimize the game result.
glossary.sentence: A beta number (β) is assigned to nodes at MIN levels. It should not increase, since this would represent a bad move.

glossary.definition: A pruning step where unconsidered paths below a node are cut off when β is less than α.
glossary.sentence: By comparing the measures, the algorithm finds that the value of β is smaller than α, meaning that an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: A pruning step where unconsidered paths below a node are cut off when α equals β.
glossary.sentence: meaning that a β-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: an approach used to process a partial state space graph based on the principles of either Minimax or Alpha-beta pruning, using a heuristic evaluation function to find the most promising moves. It explores a limited number of levels denoted by n.
glossary.sentence: The two approaches of N-ply look-ahead and iterative deepening are used to process a partial state space graph (Figure 11). They can be based on the principles of either the Minimax or Alpha-beta pruning, and use a heuristic evaluation function to find the most promising moves.

glossary.definition: an approach used when there is a time limit on making the next move in the game. It explores the partial state space graph in an iterative manner, going one level deeper at each iteration until the time limit is reached.
glossary.sentence: Iterative deepening can be used when there is a time limit on making the next move in the game. In this situation, it is not possible to use N-ply look-ahead, since a sub-graph may be so huge that the time limit could expire before it can be analysed.

glossary.definition: Paths that have not been evaluated yet in the state space graph.
