glossary.sentence: It should be noted that only unconsidered paths are cut off!

glossary.definition: A finalized heuristic measure based on all evaluated descendants or a single descendant if others are pruned.
glossary.sentence: the node whose unconsidered paths were cut off acquires a precise heuristic measure based on the heuristic measure of the only descendant evaluated so far.

glossary.definition: A heuristic measure that is not yet finalized because not all descendants have been evaluated.
glossary.sentence: When the algorithm has assigned an intermediate heuristic measure, it checks whether the ancestor of the current node in the current path also has an intermediate heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: A sequence of numbers used to represent states or moves in the game example.

glossary.definition: The intermediate heuristic measure of an ancestor node used for pruning decisions (beta).

glossary.definition: A node that is higher up in the current path from the root to the current node.

glossary.definition: a principle used in game strategies where heuristic measures are propagated back up to the node of the current state based on its rules.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm goes down from the current state of the game to the terminal nodes located at the predefined depth. It applies a heuristic evaluation function and acquires heuristic measures for the terminal nodes. Following this, the heuristic measures are propagated back up to the node of the current state, based on the principles of the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: the context in which a computer program learns for some task T and performance measure P. Its performance on T improves as measured by P.
glossary.sentence: Tom Mitchell (1997) has provided a mathematical definition: “A computer program is said to learn from experience E with respect to some task T and some performance measure P, if its performance on T, as measured by P, improves with experience E.”

glossary.definition: A task where an object is classified into one of two possible classes.
glossary.sentence: For this purpose, we can use a very simple multilayer perceptron network that includes an input layer, one hidden layer, and an output layer. The binary classification task allows a single processing unit in the output layer to classify something in two classes only.

glossary.sentence: K-nearest neighbour or kNN algorithm is a very simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks.

glossary.definition: a measure used to determine how similar data objects are, such as distance between them.
glossary.sentence: To determine the similarity of data objects, a similarity metric is used such as the distance between data objects because similar data objects are close to each other.

glossary.definition: similarity determined by comparing features of data objects.

glossary.definition: distance that can be calculated for continuous feature values.

glossary.sentence: For each data object in the dataset, the algorithm will (Fig. 17): calculate the distance (based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance) between a new data object whose output value should be determined and the current data object from the dataset;

glossary.definition: data that can be divided into distinct groups or categories.

glossary.sentence: For each data object in the dataset, the algorithm will (Fig. 17): calculate the distance (based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance) between a new data object whose output value should be determined and the current data object from the dataset;

glossary.definition: a version of kNN where only the closest data object is considered for classification.
glossary.sentence: In general, we can differentiate two versions of the algorithm. In the 1NN version (one nearest neighbour), the algorithm classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closet data object in the dataset.

glossary.definition: a problem that arises when classification is based only on one closest data object.
glossary.sentence: However, deciding the output value for a new data object based only on one closest data object leads to unstable decision boundaries.

glossary.definition: a parameter that defines the number of nearest neighbors considered in kNN algorithm.
glossary.sentence: In the kNN version of the algorithm, a hyperparameter k is used. It defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object.

glossary.definition: a process where the class label with the majority vote among the k nearest neighbors is assigned to a new data object.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm applies a voting mechanism ; that is, after finding distances of data objects in the dataset to a new data object and sorting data objects from the dataset in ascending order of the calculated distances, the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and take the plurality vote of the neighbours (which is the majority vote in the case of binary classification).

glossary.definition: the process of selecting the class label that appears most frequently among the k nearest neighbors.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm applies a voting mechanism ; that is, after finding distances of data objects in the dataset to a new data object and sorting data objects from the dataset in ascending order of the calculated distances, the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and take the plurality vote of the neighbours (which is the majority vote in the case of binary classification).

glossary.definition: a category to which a data object belongs in multiclass classification.
glossary.sentence: In this case, it is not possible to define a class for a new data object. In multiclass classification, using an odd number of nearest neighbours does not prevent ties.

glossary.definition: the closest data objects used in kNN algorithm to determine the class of a new data object.
glossary.sentence: In this case, it is not possible to define a class for a new data object. In multiclass classification, using an odd number of nearest neighbours does not prevent ties.

glossary.definition: the number of nearest neighbors considered in the kNN algorithm.
glossary.sentence: If the k value is too large, it can misclassify because unrelated data objects influence the vote (Jones, 2009). Thus, to select the value of k that fits your data, usually, it is necessary to run the algorithm several times with different k values and choose k that reduces the total error.

glossary.definition: a parameter whose value is set before the learning process begins.
glossary.sentence: Summarising the description above, it is necessary to emphasise that before you start using the algorithm, you need to define the value of the hyperparameter k , which represents the chosen number of neighbours that will be examined.

glossary.definition: the process of adjusting values measured on different scales to a common scale.

glossary.definition: the degree to which two data objects are alike.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, each cluster is made of one or more data objects and characterised by two aspects: the similarity of data objects within the cluster; the difference of data objects between the clusters.

glossary.definition: a popular unsupervised machine learning algorithm that partitions data into K clusters.
glossary.sentence: K-Means clustering The K-Means algorithm is one of the popular algorithms of unsupervised machine learning.

glossary.definition: the center point of a cluster, calculated as the average of all data points in the cluster.
glossary.sentence: A centroid is a centre of a cluster around which data objects are grouped based on their distance to the centroid. It is the average of the current set of feature vectors within the cluster (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: the number of clusters to be generated by the K-Means algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Specify the number of clusters, K , that need to be generated by this algorithm. Thus, K is the hyperparameter of this algorithm.

glossary.definition: a method of problem-solving by trying different solutions until a successful one is found.
glossary.sentence: Usually, it is selected by a trial-and-error approach.

glossary.definition: building a hierarchy of clusters that is characterised by the following aspects: the clusters at each level of the hierarchy are created by merging/dividing clusters at the next lower level; at the lowest level, each cluster contains a single data object; at the highest level, there is only one cluster containing all of the data objects.
glossary.sentence: Hierarchical clustering is about building a hierarchy of clusters that is characterised by the following aspects (Hastie, 2017) : the clusters at each level of the hierarchy are created by merging/dividing clusters at the next lower level; at the lowest level, each cluster contains a single data object; at the highest level, there is only one cluster containing all of the data objects.

glossary.definition: a bottom-up approach to building a hierarchy of clusters. Therefore, each data object initially is attributed to its own cluster. Then, at each level, a selected pair of clusters is recursively merged into a single cluster.
glossary.sentence: Agglomerative hierarchical clustering is a bottom-up approach to building a hierarchy of clusters. Therefore, each data object initially is attributed to its own cluster. Then, at each level, a selected pair of clusters is recursively merged into a single cluster.

glossary.definition: a top-down clustering approach. Therefore, it starts with a cluster with all data objects inside it. Then, at each hierarchy level, it recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters.
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach. Therefore, it starts with a cluster with all data objects inside it. Then, at each hierarchy level, it recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters.

glossary.definition: an output of hierarchical clustering. It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset. Thus, it provides insight into the way how clusters were formed.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram is an output of hierarchical clustering. It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset. According to (Hastie, 2017) , “a dendrogram provides a highly interpretable complete description of the hierarchical clustering in a graphical format” . Thus, it provides insight into the way how clusters were formed.

glossary.definition: represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height;

glossary.definition: terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): ... leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.

glossary.definition: methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters.
glossary.sentence: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods .

glossary.definition: in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.
glossary.sentence: Single-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster: where G, H – clusters; NG – the number of data objects in the cluster G; NH – the number of data objects in the cluster H; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i’ – a data object from the cluster H.
glossary.sentence: Average-linkage: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster: where G, H – clusters; NG – the number of data objects in the cluster G; NH – the number of data objects in the cluster H; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i’ – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects. It is calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset. The value lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy.
glossary.sentence: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects.

glossary.definition: a table used to describe the performance of a classification model. The main diagonal contains true positives for each class.
glossary.sentence: summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset.

glossary.definition: a poorly built model that makes predictions without considering actual patterns, often predicting a single class.
glossary.sentence: In this case, a poorly built “dummy” model which always predicts Class 0, achieves a 95% Accuracy,

glossary.definition: evaluates the fraction of predicted positives that are true positives. It is the accuracy of positive predictions. Calculated as true positives divided by (true positives + false positives). Lies in [0,1], with larger values indicating better predictive accuracy.
glossary.sentence: A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives.

glossary.definition: the sum of precisions for all classes divided by the number of classes in multiclass classification.
glossary.sentence: After that, we calculate the macro average precision for the task by summing up precisions for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes.

glossary.definition: also called sensitivity or true positive rate; evaluates the fraction of actual positives correctly classified. Calculated as true positives divided by (true positives + false negatives). Lies in [0,1], with larger values indicating better predictive accuracy.
glossary.sentence: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects.

glossary.definition: the sum of recall values for all classes divided by the number of classes in multiclass classification.
glossary.sentence: After that, we calculate the macro average recall for the task by summing up recall values for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes in the dataset.

glossary.definition: the inverse relationship between precision and recall: increasing one reduces the other.
glossary.sentence: Unfortunately, you can't have it both ways: increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa. This is called the precision/recall tradeoff.

glossary.definition: the harmonic mean of precision and recall; lies in [0,1]. High F1 indicates both high precision and recall.
glossary.sentence: The F1 score is one more metric used in the classification tasks. It represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall because the classifier will only get a high F1 score if both recall and precision are high

glossary.definition: calculated using macro average precision and recall in multiclass classification.
glossary.sentence: In the multiclass classification task, macro F1 can be calculated by the macro average precision and recall.

glossary.definition: parameters of an algorithm that are set before training begins; their values affect classifier behavior.
glossary.sentence: tuning hyperparameters of the algorithm can affect (at least to some extent) the classifier’s behaviour

glossary.definition: (The receiver operating characteristic curve) plots false positive rate (x-axis) vs true positive rate (y-axis) for different hyperparameter values, helping visualize tradeoffs. The ideal case reaches upper-left corner (0 error on FPs, 100% accuracy on TPs). Worst-case is diagonal line.
glossary.sentence: The ROC curve plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of the hyperparameter, helping you visualise values that would be good choices for the tradeoff

glossary.definition: rate of incorrect positive predictions.
glossary.sentence: The ROC curve plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis)

glossary.definition: same as recall; correct identification of positives.
glossary.sentence: The ROC curve plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis)

glossary.definition: (Area under the ROC Curve) provides a single-number summary of ROC performance. Measures area under the entire curve; ranges [0,1]. Highly accurate classifiers have AUC=1, random ones have 0.5.
glossary.sentence: Area under the ROC Curve (AUC) allows comparing different classifiers. It provides a single-number summary of the ROC curve

glossary.definition: a clustering metric that does not require ground-truth labels. Calculated using average intra-cluster distance (a) and average inter-cluster distance (b). Formula: s = (b - a)/max(a,b)
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Score is calculated by the following way: where a - average intra-cluster distance, b - average inter-cluster distance.

glossary.definition: the average distance between points within a single cluster.
glossary.sentence: where a - average intra-cluster distance (the average distance between each point within a cluster)

glossary.definition: the average distance between all clusters.
glossary.sentence: b - average inter-cluster distance (the average distance between all clusters).

glossary.definition: The model developed by Rosenblatt in the late 1950s.
glossary.sentence: Rosenblatt developed the perceptron model in the late 1950s (Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The simplest and commonly known type of artificial neural network that can learn only linearly separable functions.
glossary.sentence: A Single Layer Perceptron is the simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network, but its practical applicability is limited because it can learn only linearly separable functions.

glossary.definition: Functions that a single layer perceptron can learn.
glossary.sentence: A Single Layer Perceptron is the simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network, but its practical applicability is limited because it can learn only linearly separable functions.

glossary.definition: The layer that does not contain computing neurons and thus does not make processing.
glossary.sentence: In a single layer perceptron (Hopgood, 2001; Jones, 2009; Russell & Norvig, 2010): there is an input layer that does not contain computing neurons and thus does not make processing;

glossary.definition: The layer containing the computing neurons and providing the final output of the network.
glossary.sentence: In a single layer perceptron (Hopgood, 2001; Jones, 2009; Russell & Norvig, 2010): there is an input layer that does not contain computing neurons and thus does not make processing; there is an output layer containing the computing neurons and providing the final output of the network.

glossary.definition: The layer that processes information in a neural network. In a single layer perceptron, this is the only such layer.
glossary.sentence: The title of the network – “a single layer perceptron” – emphasizes that the network has only one processing layer; all the inputs are connected directly to the outputs.

glossary.definition: A method used to compute weight correction.
glossary.sentence: The weight correction is usually computed by applying the delta rule : Therefore, the complete rule for adjustments of the weights of the perceptron is the following one:

glossary.definition: One cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes.
glossary.sentence: An epoch represents one cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes. Therefore, the number of epochs corresponds to the number of times that the network will work through the entire training dataset.

glossary.definition: error that results from contributions of weights in all layers.
glossary.sentence: As a result, the weights of all layers contribute to the total error.

glossary.definition: A basic type of feed-forward neural network that can represent any continuous function with one hidden layer and discontinuous functions with two hidden layers.
glossary.sentence: The multilayer perceptron is a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A type of neural network that has greater processing power and can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a feed-forward neural network has greater processing power, and it can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity, where the number of inputs and the number of hidden layers determine the function complexity (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: A layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features. It is not directly observable through input/output behavior.
glossary.sentence: It has ( Negnevitsky , 2011): an input layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers. It does not include computing neurons; an output layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons; at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features.

glossary.definition: Values that are input into a neural network for processing.
glossary.sentence: Imagine that we have a dataset that contains values for two input features. Moreover, we want to use the neural network for the binary classification task.

glossary.definition: An additional input added at every layer of a neural network.
glossary.sentence: At every layer, we also add a bias input.

glossary.definition: based on steps including assigning initial random values to weights, selecting a training example from the training set, performing feed-forwarding and backpropagation of error, continuing until termination criterion is achieved.
glossary.sentence: The learning in multilayer perceptron is based on the following steps (Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011): assigning initial random values to weights; selecting a training example from the training set; performing feed-forwarding – propagation of inputs forward in the network; performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer; the training process continues until some termination criterion is achieved (see above).

glossary.definition: propagation of inputs forward in the network from the input layer to the output layer.
glossary.sentence: The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer during the feed-forwarding .

glossary.definition: algorithm that allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of error from the output layer to the input layer.
glossary.sentence: The backpropagation algorithm allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer (Fig. The algorithm uses two types of errors: the first is used for output neurons, and the second is used only for neurons in hidden layers.

glossary.definition: dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels, so by feeding a particular data object into the network, we expect to acquire a known output.
glossary.sentence: In a supervised machine learning task, the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels. It means that by feeding a particular data object into the network, we expect to acquire a known output.

glossary.definition: difference between the right output and the predicted output.
glossary.sentence: However, in reality, the output predicted by the network differs from the actual output. Thus, there is a difference between the right output and the predicted output. It is called an error.

glossary.definition: units whose values are not directly provided by training data; each is responsible for some fraction of the error in each of the output units.
glossary.sentence: The training data do not say what value the hidden processing units should have, and each hidden processing unit is responsible for some fraction of the error in each of the output units (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: neurons for which error is calculated using predicted output and real class label.
glossary.sentence: In the output neuron, we know the predicted output and the real class label and, thus, can calculate the error in the following way:

glossary.definition: neurons for which error is calculated differently than for output neurons.
glossary.sentence: In the hidden layers, the error for each processing unit is calculated in the following way:

glossary.definition: intelligent computer system containing a knowledge base and inference engine.
glossary.sentence: In the field of artificial intelligence, knowledge representation is needed to develop knowledge-based intelligent computer systems. A knowledge-based intelligent system is an intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: the structure where the rules used by human experts must be preserved when building it.
glossary.sentence: the structure of rules used by the human experts must be preserved in building a knowledge base

glossary.definition: the processing mechanism that handles the knowledge stored in the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: Separation of knowledge and control. Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine.

glossary.definition: The average time taken for people to respond to a question.
glossary.sentence: the mean reaction time to the question “Can a canary bird fly?” is higher than the mean reaction time to the question “Can a canary bird sing?”

glossary.definition: The structured route in memory that people follow to find an answer by moving from specific concepts to broader ones.
glossary.sentence: people need to move along the hierarchical path in the structure of the memory.

glossary.definition: Knowledge stored at the level of a concept it belongs to.
glossary.sentence: the researchers also have revealed that exceptional knowledge is stored at the level of a concept it belongs to.

glossary.definition: A knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph with labelled nodes corresponding to concepts and directed arcs representing relations between them.
glossary.sentence: Collins and Quillian created a semantic network that is displayed in Figure 4. (the text fragment is based on information given in (Luger, 2009)) A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them

glossary.definition: A hierarchical relation represented by 'is a' that links narrower concepts to broader ones.
glossary.sentence: A relation “subclass-class” (also “subset-set”, “subtype-type”, “ subkind -kind”) is represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”). It allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts

glossary.definition: A hierarchical relation represented by 'is an instance/example of' that links specific objects to the classes they belong to.
glossary.sentence: A relation “instance-class/set/type/kind” is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”. It also allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to

glossary.definition: A relation represented by 'Part of' that links parts of an object to the whole object.
glossary.sentence: A relation “part-whole” is represented by the title of “Part of”. It allows relating parts of the object to the whole object. Without the defined parts, the object will lose some important properties or its original appearance.

glossary.definition: A relation represented by 'property/has a property of' that links objects to their characteristics.
glossary.sentence: A relation “object-property” is represented by the title of “property/has a property of”. It allows relating objects/concepts to their properties

glossary.definition: A relation represented by 'value' that links properties to their specific values.
glossary.sentence: A relation “property-value” is represented by the title of “value”. It allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values. This relation can come only after the relation “property/has a property of”.

glossary.definition: Relations that specify any other kind of connections not covered by predefined types like 'like', 'own', or 'travel'.
glossary.sentence: Linguistic relations (Fig.11). They allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.

glossary.definition: the mechanism by which sub-classes and instances acquire attributes from their parent classes.
glossary.sentence: The generalization relation is defined between TreeAlex-Person , BrownLucie-Person , Tom-Cat , Rex-Dog , Cat-Animal , Dog-Animal .

glossary.definition: Knowledge about facts, objects, and concepts that semantic networks can represent.
glossary.sentence: they represent only declarative knowledge (knowledge about facts, objects, and concepts )

glossary.definition: a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system.
glossary.sentence: A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: procedures that implement actions in a frame-based expert system.
glossary.sentence: Demons implement actions in a frame-based expert system (Jones, 2009; Luger,2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: a type of expert system that uses frames to represent knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Demons implement actions in a frame-based expert system (Jones, 2009; Luger,2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: a place in a frame where specific information is stored.
glossary.sentence: Demons are triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed (Mallach, 1994).

glossary.definition: a demon that is triggered when the slot value is unspecified and needs to be calculated or set.
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it;

glossary.definition: a demon that is triggered when a slot value changes.
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes;

glossary.definition: a demon that is triggered when a slot value is unspecified and added to the frame.
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame;

glossary.definition: a demon that is triggered when a slot value is deleted.
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-REMOVED – it is triggered when a slot value is deleted.

glossary.definition: a template that defines attributes (slots) and demons for a category of objects.
glossary.sentence: Demons are usually defined in class and sub-class frames and then inherited and executed in instance frames.

glossary.definition: a frame that inherits attributes from a parent class frame and may have additional slots.
glossary.sentence: Next, there are two sub-class frames: Cat and Dog . They inherit slots from the Animal class frame,

glossary.definition: a specific example of a class or sub-class that contains actual data.
glossary.sentence: Finally, there are four instance frames: TreeAlex and BrownLucie are instances for the Person class frame; Tom is an instance for the Cat class frame; Rex is an instance for the Dog class frame.

glossary.definition: links between frames that represent relationships like ownership or fear.
glossary.sentence: The frame system also represents the following associative relations between frames: BrownLucie - Tom , the relation Owns , and Tom- BrownLucie , the relation Animal owner;

glossary.definition: a type of intelligent computer system that can combine both search directions: data-driven and goal-driven.
glossary.sentence: It is possible to combine both search directions in a rule-based expert system ( Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: a chunk of knowledge for solving problems presented as IF condition THEN consequence.
glossary.sentence: Each production rule is a chunk of knowledge about solving a problem (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: an intelligent system that uses rules with statements in the THEN part to make logical deductions.
glossary.sentence: According to (Winston, 1992), if statements are added to the THEN part of rules, then a deductive intelligent system is acquired;

glossary.definition: an intelligent system that uses rules with actions in the THEN part to respond to conditions.
glossary.sentence: However, if the THEN part of rules contains actions, then a reactive intelligent system is acquired.

glossary.definition: rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts: a knowledge base that contains a set of production rules; a working memory; an inference engine.
glossary.sentence: Production systems are rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2005; Negnevitsky , 2011): a knowledge base that contains a set of production rules; a working memory; an inference engine.

glossary.definition: a memory where facts are stored with time tags for determining recency.
glossary.sentence: fire the rule that uses the data most recently entered in the working memory. This method relies on time tags attached to each fact in the working memory;

glossary.definition: essential for any expert system because it provides the user with an explanation of how a particular conclusion was reached and justifies the output.
glossary.sentence: Explanation facilities are essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result. However, to accept the system’s recommendation, the human expert should be aware of how a particular conclusion was reached, what was the line of reasoning and whether it is valid or not (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: the means through which the user interacts with the system, designed as a question-and-answer, menu-driven, natural language, or graphical interface.
glossary.sentence: The user interacts with the system through a user interface designed as a question-and-answer, menu-driven, natural language, or graphical interface (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: additional components that may be added to an expert system to allow it to work with external data files and program results.
glossary.sentence: Additional components, such as external applications and developer interface, can be added as well.

glossary.definition: intended to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules as well as modify and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules.
glossary.sentence: The developer interface is intended to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules as well as modify and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: data that are assumed to be correct when used, separated from the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The case-specific data are separated from the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). These data are assumed to be correct when they are used (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: a description of the current state of the problem-solving process stored in the working memory.
glossary.sentence: The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern.

glossary.definition: the basis of the inference mechanism that implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by matching the content of the working memory with production rules.
glossary.sentence: The control cycle “Recognize-act” makes the basis of the inference mechanism. It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.

glossary.definition: a process where the control cycle matches the pattern in the working memory against the IF or THEN part of all rules available in the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules. While implementing the pattern-directed search, the control cycle performs the following steps: the working memory contains a pattern corresponding to the current state of the problem-solving process; the pattern is matched against the IF or THEN part (it depends on the search direction) of all rules available in the knowledge base;

glossary.definition: the set of enabled rules from which one rule is chosen to fire during conflict resolution.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: the process of selecting one rule from the conflict set for firing using a conflict resolution strategy.
glossary.sentence: The selection of one rule from the conflict set is called conflict resolution. For this purpose, a conflict resolution strategy is used;

glossary.definition: the process of applying a selected rule that changes the content of the working memory by adding its IF or THEN part (depending on the search direction).
glossary.sentence: one of the enabled rules is fired, and it changes the content of the working memory because its IF or THEN part is added to the working memory (depending on the search direction);

glossary.definition: methods used to make the search process more effective in rule-based expert systems, including control through the selection of search direction, rule sequence and structure, and conflict resolution strategy.
glossary.sentence: Three search control strategies are used for this purpose (Luger, 2009): control through the selection of search direction; control through the selection of rule sequence and structure; control through the selection of conflict resolution strategy.

glossary.definition: an inference engine chosen when a domain expert first gathers information and then tries to infer from it.
glossary.sentence: If a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred, choose the forward chaining inference engine

glossary.definition: an inference engine chosen when a domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and attempts to find facts to prove it.
glossary.sentence: if your domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and then attempts to find facts to prove it, choose the backward chaining inference engine

glossary.definition: rules used by human experts that specify conditions for successful problem solving.
glossary.sentence: problem domain experts use conditions specified in rules in a certain order and also apply rules in a certain order. Such an approach determines the conditions for successful problem solving.

glossary.definition: a method that determines how to choose one rule from the set of enabled rules (conflict set).
glossary.sentence: Both the structure and the sequence of production rules are significant in the context of a chosen conflict resolution strategy.

glossary.definition: an operator that introduces ambiguity and should be avoided.
glossary.sentence: try avoiding the use of OR in production rules because this operator introduces ambiguity;

glossary.term: AND and OR in the same rule
glossary.definition: combinations that should be avoided to prevent ambiguity.
glossary.sentence: if it is not possible to avoid using OR, try avoiding the use of combinations of AND and OR in the same rule;

glossary.definition: the part where only one action or statement/conclusion should be added.
glossary.sentence: try adding only one action or statement/conclusion to the THEN part of production rules.

glossary.term: fire the first/the last rule found
glossary.definition: a conflict resolution strategy where the order of examining rules determines which rule is fired without assembling a conflict set.
glossary.sentence: to fire the first/the last rule found. In this scheme, the conflict set is not assembled at all, and the order in which rules are selected for examination determines conflict resolution (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.term: fire rules based on their priorities
glossary.definition: a strategy where rules with explicitly stated priority values are chosen, with the highest priority rule being fired if multiple can fire.
glossary.sentence: to fire rules based on their priorities. Rules can be written so that each has an explicitly stated priority value, and, thus, if more than one rule can fire, the one with the highest priority is chosen (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: a strategy also known as the longest matching strategy that fires rules processing more information.
glossary.sentence: to fire more specific rules. This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: a conflict resolution method where more specific rules, which have more conditions, are fired.
glossary.sentence: This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: production rules as a natural way because human experts explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...”.
glossary.sentence: Production rules are a natural way of knowledge representation because human experts very often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...” ( Negnevitsky , 2011 )

glossary.definition: production rules offer a unified way of storing knowledge, with each rule being an independent unit.
glossary.sentence: Uniform structure. Production rules offer a unified way of storing knowledge because each rule is an independent unit of knowledge;

glossary.definition: knowledge stored in the knowledge base, processed by the inference engine, allowing modification without changing code.
glossary.sentence: Separation of knowledge and control. Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine. The advantages of this separation include ease of modifying the knowledge base without requiring a change in the code for program control and, conversely, the ability to alter the code for program control without changing the set of production rules (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 200; Negnevitsky , 2011)

glossary.definition: rules have no syntactic interactions and only affect firing by changing working memory patterns.
glossary.sentence: Modularity of production rules. Rules have no syntactic interactions. Rules may only affect the firing of other rules by changing the pattern in working memory;

glossary.definition: the modularity and iterative execution of rules make it easier to trace and explain system actions.
glossary.sentence: Tracing and explanation. The modularity of rules and the iterative nature of their execution make it easier to trace the execution of production rules. At each stage of the control cycle "Recognize-act", the selected rule can be displayed.

glossary.definition: factors added to represent and reason with incomplete and uncertain knowledge in expert systems.
glossary.sentence: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.

glossary.definition: the cycle where at each stage, the selected rule can be displayed for tracing.
glossary.sentence: At each stage of the control cycle "Recognize-act", the selected rule can be displayed.
